text:
Department of Neurology 

Clinic Date: 09 March 2021 

Dr Aisha Rahman
St George’s University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust
Atkinson Morley Neuroscience Centre, Blackshaw Road, London SW17 0QT

Dear Dr Aisha Rahman,

Michael Thompson, DOB: 23-02-1991, Hospital No: H392774 NHS No. 9045128837
18 Larkspur Close, Wimbledon, London SW19 6JP

Diagnoses:

Primary generalised epilepsy with generalised spike-and-wave on prior EEG; history of circadian disruption after trans-meridian travel with perceived worsening in seizure control.

Current Medication:

- Valproate (Sodium Valproate) 500 mg mane and 1000 mg nocte
- Levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily
- Rescue: Buccal midazolam 10 mg as needed for prolonged convulsions (>5 minutes)

Previously tried/ceased due to tolerability or suboptimal response:

- Topiramate (paresthesias and cognitive slowing)
- Lamotrigine (photosensitive rash)
- Clobazam (sedation on daytime dosing)

Plan:

- Reinforce sleep hygiene and time-zone transition strategy; discuss travel risk management and safety planning
- Arrange repeat EEG and serum drug levels (valproate trough, levetiracetam) and check bone profile, LFTs, FBC
- Provide updated seizure action plan for patient and family; advise to notify DVLA if applicable
- Expedite review in 3 months or sooner if further deterioration

I reviewed Michael Thompson in clinic today following recent travel across multiple time zones. He reports significant jet lag with fragmented sleep and higher perceived seizure burden since return. He still has focal clonic 4 times per day, drop attacks occurring in batches, and tonic-clonic seizures 2 times per month, this long-standing pattern has persisted without major change; however, he and his partner feel that recovery after events is slower and that clusters are occurring closer together following nights of curtailed sleep. There has been one near-injury during a drop attack at home without head strike. No status epilepticus.

He has known generalised epilepsy confirmed previously by EEG demonstrating generalised spike-and-wave activity. There is no new intercurrent illness, alcohol excess, or missed doses reported, though he acknowledges irregular dosing times during travel. Examination today is unremarkable with normal cardiorespiratory observations and no focal neurological deficit between seizures.

We discussed the relationship between circadian disruption and seizure threshold. I advised stabilising wake–sleep times, gradual clock-shifting before long-haul flights, and using alarms to maintain regular dosing when travelling. I will check antiepileptic drug levels and repeat an EEG to assess for any change in background generalised epileptiform activity. At present, he wishes to continue current therapy; we will revisit options including cautious dose optimisation or adjunctive therapy if objective deterioration is confirmed.

I have provided an updated written seizure plan including use of buccal midazolam for prolonged convulsive seizures and guidance for his family on cluster management. He understands to seek urgent care for any seizure lasting longer than five minutes, repeated seizures without recovery, or new injuries.

I will review him in three months, or earlier if there is any further worsening. Please do not hesitate to contact me if you have any concerns in the interim.

With best wishes,

entities.text: He still has focal clonic 4 times per day, drop attacks occurring in batches, and tonic-clonic seizures 2 times per month
He still has focal clonic 4 times per day, drop attacks occurring in batches, and tonic-clonic seizures 2 times per month, this long-standing pattern has persisted without major change

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Identify explicit frequencies mentioned. The letter states: 'He still has focal clonic 4 times per day, drop attacks occurring in batches, and tonic-clonic seizures 2 times per month.' Apply the rule to select the highest frequency across types. The highest explicit frequency is '4 times per day' for focal clonic seizures. Convert to allowed format: '4 per day'. No need to sum different seizure types; rule says pick the highest frequency when multiple exist. Include a verbatim quote supporting the frequency.
